summary: The St. Johns River has always been a majestic presence near the estate. From the crest of the hill, I could see sail-rigged lumber ships and black-bowed freighters passing by, one heading into Jacksonville's port and the other bound for Charleston, Baltimore, and New York. The riverbank has changed over time, especially after the United States government's dredging operations extended the shoreline by about a hundred feet, creating new fertile land. The old pump-house and dock by the river, once used by the Cummings' private yacht, have sadly fallen into decay.

Mrs. Mary Packer Cummings was the former owner of the estate on the south side of the St. Johns River, which she willed unconditionally to St. Johns Episcopal Church of Jacksonville. She was passionately fond of roses and spent many hours tending to her rose beds on the property. A bronze tablet she placed on the big oak tree there speaks to her appreciation of nature's grandeur. Her legacy lives on as the estate now serves as a home for orphan boys under the care of the church.

St. Johns Episcopal Church of Jacksonville became the caretaker of the estate after Mrs. Mary Packer Cummings willed it to them. The church uses the property as a home for orphan boys from the parish, with the Rev. and Mrs. A. M. Blackford presiding over their care. The boys attend South Jacksonville public schools, traveling daily from the estate six miles out along Atlantic Beach Highway. The church's stewardship has preserved much of the estate's history and provided a nurturing environment for these children.
cues: Rev. and Mrs. A. M. Blackford

summary: Jacksonville, Florida, is the city near which the estate is located, about six miles from the business section of South Jacksonville. The estate overlooks the St. Johns River, a vital waterway for the city’s commerce, with ships coming in and out of its port. The Atlantic Beach Highway connects the estate to Jacksonville, and the city’s public schools serve the boys living on the property. Jacksonville's history and growth are reflected in the changing landscape around the estate.
cues: six miles from South Jacksonville business section

summary: The estate is approached by a quarter-mile drive from Atlantic Beach Highway, which lies six miles out from South Jacksonville's business district. Each morning, the boys living on the estate walk to this highway to catch the bus that takes them to their public schools. This highway serves as a vital link between the estate and the surrounding communities. It also marks the boundary from which the estate’s long driveway begins.

Rev. A. M. Blackford, along with his wife, presides over the orphan boys living at the estate given to St. Johns Episcopal Church. He has a particular love for flowers, especially calla lilies, which he cultivates with great success, bringing both fame and fortune. His care extends beyond the boys to the estate’s gardens, where blooming rose bushes and other flowers flourish under his attention. He plays a central role in maintaining the estate’s legacy and nurturing the children.

On the estate, among the remnants of an old orange grove, there are several satsuma trees growing alongside pear trees. These citrus trees are part of the estate’s agricultural heritage and add to the natural beauty of the grounds. Their presence hints at the estate’s past as a productive and well-tended property. The satsumas stand as a reminder of the estate’s connection to Florida’s citrus culture.

Charleston, South Carolina, is one of the destinations for freighters passing by the estate on the St. Johns River. I often watched black-bowed freighters bound outward with cargo for Charleston, Baltimore, and New York. This connection highlights the estate’s proximity to important shipping routes and commerce along the eastern seaboard. It reminds me of the broader world beyond the estate’s grounds.
cues: freighters passing on St. Johns River

summary: Baltimore is another key port city to which ships departing from Jacksonville’s port carry cargo. From the estate’s vantage point on the St. Johns River, I could see freighters heading towards Baltimore, alongside those bound for Charleston and New York. This illustrates the estate’s location near important maritime trade routes. The flow of commerce through these ports connects the estate’s quiet grounds to bustling cities far away.

New York City is one of the destinations for ships leaving Jacksonville’s port, visible from the estate on the St. Johns River. Watching the freighters bound for New York reminded me of the vast network of commerce linking this southern estate to the northern cities. It’s remarkable to see such a connection from a quiet place six miles out from South Jacksonville. The estate’s location offers a unique perspective on the movement of goods along the eastern coast.

The United States government played a role in reshaping the estate’s shoreline through dredging operations on the St. Johns River to deepen the channel. The sand and silt pumped up during these operations extended the shoreline by about a hundred feet, which was then bulkheaded and fertilized to create a large vegetable garden. This government intervention changed the landscape significantly and contributed to the estate’s agricultural productivity. It’s an example of how federal projects impacted local environments.
